title: Pierogi z kapustą i ziemniakami
subtitle: Polish dumplings with sauerkraut and potato
excerpt: Sturdy, satisfying dumplings filled with mashed potato and sauerkraut, boiled then pan-fried in butter with caramelised onion. One of the most popular pierogi fillings in Poland, tangy, earthy, and complete.
sourceNotes: The sauerkraut-and-potato filling (kapusta z ziemniakami) is one of the two or three fillings that define Polish pierogi. Along with the mushroom-and-sauerkraut Wigilia version, this is the filling most Polish families make most often. The combination of fermented cabbage and starchy potato produces a filling that is both rich and sharp, the sauerkraut cuts through the potato in a way that keeps each bite interesting.

content.text: The potato-and-sauerkraut filling is the most forgiving of the classic pierogi fillings: it is easy to make, holds together well, and is much harder to overfill than mushroom or fruit. The key is to cook the sauerkraut until all its moisture has gone before mixing it with the mashed potato. Wet filling tears the dough and bursts the seal.

Pan-frying in butter after boiling is not optional: it gives the dumplings a golden crust on one side and deepens the flavour substantially. A few minutes in a hot pan with caramelised onion transforms a boiled dumpling into something much better.

Boil the {{potato}} in salted water until completely tender, 20 to 25 minutes. Drain well and mash until smooth. Cook the {{onion}} in the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium heat for 10 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{sauerkraut}} and cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until dry. Mix the sauerkraut and onion into the mashed potato. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Leave to cool completely before using.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{eggs}}, {{soured-cream}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until a dough forms. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes. Roll out to 2 mm on a floured surface. Cut rounds using an 8 cm cutter. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each round. Fold and press edges to seal firmly. Place on a floured tray.

Boil in batches in well-salted water. They are done 2 to 3 minutes after floating to the surface. Remove with a slotted spoon. Meanwhile, melt the remaining {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide frying pan over medium heat. Cook the sliced {{onion}} for 10 minutes until golden. Add the cooked pierogi and fry for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until one side is golden. Serve with soured cream.

items.fix: Press the sealed edge firmly and crimp with a fork. If the filling is wet, spread it on a tray and refrigerate for 20 minutes to firm up before using
items.cause: Seal not firm enough or filling too wet
items.symptom: Filling leaking into the cooking water

items.fix: Cook in batches of 8 to 10. Stir gently immediately after adding to prevent sticking to the bottom
items.cause: Too many in the pot at once
items.symptom: Dumplings sticking together in the water

items.fix: Use a wide pan and do not crowd it. The butter should be sizzling before the pierogi go in
items.cause: Pan not hot enough or too much butter causing steaming rather than frying

content.text: These pierogi are everyday food in Poland. Unlike the Wigilia mushroom pierogi, which are reserved for Christmas Eve, the potato-and-sauerkraut version is made throughout the year. They are sold at milk bars, at street stalls, at Christmas markets, and at every kind of family occasion. Made in large batches at the weekend, they sustain a family through several meals, boiled the first day, pan-fried in butter the second, and served cold at the table in both configurations.
